case_name: Estate of Effie Taylor v. University Physician Group

summary.brief: Estate of Effie Taylor v. University Physician Group. Medical malpractice case involving cardiology.

evidence_index.text: This medical malpractice case arises from a colonoscopy performed by defendant Manuel Sklar, M.D., on plaintiff’s decedent, Effie Taylor.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: During the procedure, Dr. Sklar observed lesions in Taylor’s colon that he believed were arteriovenous malformations, called AVMs.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Three days later, Taylor developed colorectal bleeding. Despite the emergent removal of her entire colon, Taylor died.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Plaintiff’s expert witness, Dr. Todd Eisner, testified that the improper and unindicated biopsies caused the bleeding that ultimately led to Taylor’s death.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Todd Eisner

evidence_index.text: His expert witness, Dr. Veslav Stecevic, performed an emergent colonoscopy on Taylor the day before she died, looking for the source of the bleeding in her colon.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Veslav Stecevic

evidence_index.text: Dr. Stecevic recorded that he found '[r]ed blood . . . in the entire colon' during the second colonoscopy, and performed a '[l]imited exam due to large amount of blood in the entire colon.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Eisner explained that Dr. Sklar’s description of the lesion he biopsied matched an AVM, and that it is common for AVMs to be found in the right colon.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Todd Eisner

evidence_index.text: The trial court denied defendants’ motion, stating, 'The Court finds that summary disposition is not appropriate.'
evidence_index.speaker: Trial court

evidence_index.text: Summary disposition is improper when a trier of fact could reasonably draw an inference supporting causation from the established facts.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: Dr. Sklar acknowledged awareness that Taylor, a 79-year-old woman and a Jehovah’s Witness, had been taking Plavix before the colonoscopy.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Sklar dictated the official operative report on the day of the colonoscopy. He noted that a segment of Taylor’s ascending colon 'had an appearance of multiple small blood vessels suggestive for an extensive AVM malformation.'
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Sklar

evidence_index.text: Dr. Eisner explained that diverticular bleeding is 'very rare, however old you are,' and is not a reported complication of a colonoscopy.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Todd Eisner

evidence_index.text: We affirm and remand for further proceedings. We do not retain jurisdiction.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient had been taking Plavix, a blood thinner, and was instructed to discontinue it 5-7 days before the procedure but stopped only 3 days prior.

reveal.result_summary: Lesions observed, suspected to be AVMs
reveal.clinical_significance: Potential source of bleeding if biopsied
reveal.decision_impact: Biopsy decision critical due to bleeding risk

reveal.clinical_significance: Biopsying AVMs in a patient on Plavix is risky
reveal.decision_impact: Informed consent and biopsy decision questioned

key_events.event: Patient instructed to stop Plavix 5-7 days before procedure
Emergent colonoscopy performed by Dr. Stecevic

decision_points.clinical_context: During colonoscopy, lesions suspected to be AVMs were observed
decision_points.prompt: Should biopsies be taken from the suspected AVMs?

options.description: Do not biopsy due to bleeding risk
options.clinical_reasoning: Patient on Plavix and Jehovah’s Witness, high bleeding risk

actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Believed lesions were AVMs and needed confirmation

expected_action_court.description: Biopsies should not have been taken
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated high risk of bleeding

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Biopsying AVMs in a patient on Plavix is risky and not standard care

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed massive colorectal bleeding and died after total colectomy

key_findings: Breach of duty in performing biopsies on AVMs
Causation established - biopsies led to fatal bleeding

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Biopsying AVMs in a patient on Plavix
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Avoiding biopsies would have prevented bleeding

tests_performed.result: Lesions suspected to be AVMs biopsied

diagnoses.diagnosis: Colorectal bleeding due to biopsied AVMs

court_findings.finding: Defendant breached duty of care by performing biopsies
